title: Chapter 33: The Storage Unit
content: Ten months after the Choice, I'm back in New York.
Not to stay. Just to close the door.

Julian walks beside me through Times Square with his head on a swivel, and it might be the best thing I've ever seen. His eyes are wide, his mouth slightly open, spinning between the digital billboards and the costumed characters and the taxi horns and a man screaming about Jesus into a megaphone. He looks like someone dropped a sheltered Victorian time traveler into the center of the universe and said "good luck."
"How do any of you think straight," he says, gesturing at literally everything — the lights, the noise, the entire sensory assault of Broadway after dark — "with all this — happening?"
"We don't," I say. "That's the secret. Nobody here is thinking straight. We're all just vibrating at the same frequency until it feels normal."
He stares at a forty-foot shoe advertisement cycling through colors I'm not sure exist in Ireland. "This explains so much about you."

I laugh, and the sound gets swallowed by a passing double-decker tourist bus blasting despacito at a volume that feels legally actionable. Julian flinches, then laughs too, and I pull him north because if we stand here any longer his retinas will need therapy.

We're staying in Midtown — just two nights, just long enough. The Murray Hill apartment is already gone. Sublet ended in March, keys mailed back to the landlord, the clause in the lease about early termination negotiated via email from Clonakilty at two in the morning with spotty Wi-Fi and a cup of tea that kept going cold. I didn't go see it. There was nothing to see. Someone else's furniture in there now, someone else's life layered over mine like fresh paint over old wallpaper.

But the storage unit in Chelsea still has my name on it. Literally — there's a plastic tag on the roll-up door that says CHEN, A. in faded Sharpie. I've been paying the monthly fee automatically, the charge hitting my checking account with the same unremarkable rhythm as a subscription I forgot to cancel. Which, I suppose, is exactly what it is.

Chelsea Self-Storage is on a block between a wine bar and a deli that smells like pastrami and ambition. The elevator is small and groans like it has opinions about carrying people to their past. Julian and I squeeze in together, and he watches me press the button for the third floor with an expression I can't quite read.
"You don't have to come in," I say.
"I know."
"You've never been to New York before. There are probably better things to do than watch me sort through old boxes."
"Avery." He looks at me with those steady dark eyes. "I've spent the last several years hiding in a house in west Cork. I think I can handle a storage unit."

The elevator shudders to a stop. The doors open onto a corridor of identical roll-up doors, each one someone's archive, someone's pause button, someone's I'll deal with it later. The fluorescent lights hum the way fluorescent lights always hum in places like this — tuneless, persistent, a little bit threatening.
Unit 317. I fit the key into the padlock and the mechanism resists, stiff from months of neglect, and then gives. I roll up the door.

Dust hits me first. That specific, stale, sealed-room dust — dry and faintly metallic on my tongue, like the air forgot how to circulate. Then the visual: a ten-by-ten-foot rectangle packed with my former life, stacked and labeled in handwriting I barely recognize as my own. Boxes on boxes, a garment bag hanging from a hook on the side wall, two framed things I can't identify from here, a floor lamp I definitely don't remember storing.

Julian steps in beside me. The unit is small enough that we're immediately in it, surrounded, the walls close. He doesn't say anything. He just takes it in: the careful labels, the neat stacks, the OCD-level organization of someone who could not, under any circumstances, let go.
"She was thorough," he says finally.
"She was afraid," I correct.

I crouch down and start with the nearest box. Kitchen things — a French press I bought during a phase, a set of measuring spoons, a ceramic bowl with a chip in it that I kept because it was the first thing I bought after moving to New York. I hold the bowl, turning it over, running my thumb along the chip. I remember saving for it. I remember the satisfaction of carrying it home on the subway, holding it in my lap like a small triumph.
I set it in the donate pile.

The garment bag is next. I unzip it and the scent that rises is chemical and sharp — dry cleaning solvent, still clinging to the fabric after all this time. Inside: three suits. Not just any suits. The suits. The ones I bought at Saks with the Sterling Capital signing bonus, the ones that cost more than my mother's first car, the ones that were supposed to say I belong here with the authority of a woman who knows exactly who she is.
They look smaller than I remember. Not physically smaller. Just... less.
"Those are intense," Julian says, reading the label on one of the jackets.

"Sterling Capital required a certain aesthetic." I say it flatly, the way you describe a weather event that happened to you. "Power dressing. Minimalist, no-nonsense. The uniform of people who confuse expensive fabric with competence."
"Did it work?"
"I got promoted twice, so." I shrug. "Either it worked or I'm actually good at my job."
"Could be both."
I smile. "Could be."

The suits go in the donate pile. All three of them. I don't even hesitate, and that's how I know I've changed, because the old Avery would have debated each one for twenty minutes, running mental calculations about hypothetical scenarios where she might need a navy blazer, constructing elaborate justification ladders for keeping things she would never wear again. The new Avery — the Clonakilty Avery, the who-knows-what-comes-next Avery — she just lets them go.

I move deeper into the unit. Behind the kitchen boxes and the garment bag, pushed against the back wall, there's a box labeled OLD STUFF — PHONE, COLLEGE, MISC in aggressive Sharpie caps, like past-me was leaving a warning for future-me.
I open it.

My old phone is right on top. The one from before. The one that doesn't have the Fork app because the Fork app came after, because there was a before and an after and this phone is the border between them. It's a black rectangle, screen cracked in one corner from a drop I don't remember, and it feels impossibly light in my hand — lighter than phones are now, lighter than memory said it was.

I find the charger in the box beneath it, plug it into the wall socket that every storage unit has for reasons no one has ever adequately explained, and wait. The dead battery screen appears. Then, slowly, the apple logo. Then, the lock screen.
I tap in the passcode. My fingers remember it even though my brain doesn't: 0417. April seventeenth. Mika's birthday. I haven't thought about that in — I don't know how long.
The photos app. I open it and scroll.

There she is. Graduation day, Columbia regalia, the silly hat, the impossible grin. Mika beside her, pushing her face into the frame with her eyes crossed and her tongue out, doing what Mika always did which was make sure no moment was too serious to survive. Her parents on either side of her, her mother's hand on her shoulder, her father's smile — careful, controlled, proud in the way that his generation equated with restraint.

And there — selfie in the bathroom of her first New York apartment, the one with the bad lighting and the dripping faucet, and she's smiling. Not performing. Not posing for a future version of herself who might be scrolling through old photos looking for evidence that she was once happy. Just smiling because she was.

I stare at the before-Avery. The one who hadn't chosen anything yet, the one standing at the fork before the fork existed, the one who thought her biggest decision would be which equity research team to join. She is specific and whole and completely unknown to me now.
I don't miss her.

That sounds harsh, maybe. Or sad. But it isn't. I don't miss her the way you don't miss a summer camp friend you were close to once and grew apart from naturally. You remember the inside jokes. You remember the bug spray and the bonfire and the way the lake looked at 6 a.m. You'd be happy to see her again. But you're not the same person who needed her, and she's not waiting for you to come back.
I put the phone in the keep pile.

"What's that?" Julian asks, peering at a frame I haven't noticed him pick up. It's my Sterling Capital ID badge, blown up and framed — some corporate onboarding artifact I apparently thought worth preserving. My face in the photo is composed to within an inch of its life: chin up, mouth a straight line, eyes communicating something between determination and a hostage situation.
Julian holds it up and studies it with the focus of an art critic evaluating a particularly troubling portrait.
"You look like you're trying to murder someone with your mind," he says.
I laugh — really laugh, the kind that comes from somewhere low and involuntary. "I was trying to look competent."
"Same thing, in corporate."

He puts the badge down and rummages through the box beside it. His hand emerges holding a small object, and I see it before he does — a keychain, a cheap silver thing attached to a ring of keys I don't use anymore. And on the keychain: a fork. An actual fork. Like for eating. A novelty keychain someone — Mika, it was Mika, she gave it to me the night we got dumplings in the East Village, she said "in case you ever need to fork something up" and I groaned so hard she made me keep it as punishment — a fork.
Julian looks at the keychain. He looks at me. I look at the keychain. I look at him.
The irony lands on both of us at exactly the same instant.

He starts laughing first — a bark of a laugh, the kind he does when something catches him off-guard — and then I'm laughing, really laughing, doubled-over, tears-pricking laughing, the fork keychain dangling from his fingers between us, and we're in this fluorescent-lit closet of a storage unit surrounded by the debris of a life I don't live anymore and the universe has a sense of humor after all.
"A fork," Julian manages, holding it up like a medieval relic.
"A fork," I confirm, wiping my eyes.
"For eating."
"For eating."

He dangles it and it catches the ugly light and swings, and I laugh until my ribs ache, and somewhere in the sound of it I hear something release — some last pocket of air I'd been holding, some final pressure valve on the part of me that was still afraid to find any of this funny.
We settle. I take the keychain from him and roll it in my palm. The metal is cool and light and ridiculous. I put it in the keep pile.
Then I start deciding.

The degrees — framed, under glass, Columbia and NYU Stern — I put in the keep pile. Not because I need them on a wall, but because they're proof of something I built before I knew what I was building. The suits — already donate, the fabric already belonging to someone else's interview, someone else's Friday confidence. The awards — the Sterling Capital President's Club, the 30 Under 30 from some finance magazine I can't remember the name of — I photograph each one, front and back, with my phone. Then they go in the donate pile. I don't need the objects. I just need the proof that I earned them, and a photo is proof enough.

The kitchen things: donate. The floor lamp: donate. The ceramic bowl with the chip — I pause over it again, then add it to the donate pile next to the suits. The old phone: keep. The fork keychain: keep. A scarf my mother gave me: keep. A stack of books I'll never reread: donate. A pair of running shoes with the soles worn through: trash.

It's the opposite of what I would have done. The before-Avery, the one in the photos, the one who hadn't chosen anything yet — she kept everything. Every receipt, every ticket stub, every card and note and scrap of evidence that she existed in particular moments. She was afraid that throwing away the coffee receipt from the bodega on 32nd Street might throw away the person she was while she drank that coffee. That discarding the concert program meant discarding the version of herself who sang every word in the third row. She treated objects like memory — like the self was something stored in things, and without the things, the self might dissolve.
But that's not how it works. You don't lose yourself by letting go. You make room for whoever you're becoming. The proof isn't in the object. It's in the person who holds it, and then puts it down.

I fill three bags for donation. Two for trash. The keep pile fits in one box — small, light, manageable. The girl who needed a ten-by-ten storage unit to hold her identity is gone. In her place is someone who can carry everything that matters in one trip.

Julian watches me work without comment. He opens boxes when I point to them, holds things up so I can see what's inside, piles the donation bags near the door. He doesn't offer opinions or ask if I'm sure. He knows this isn't a sure kind of process. It's a knowing kind.

When the unit is empty — really empty, just dust outlines on the shelves where boxes used to sit — I roll down the door and lock the padlock for the last time. The key goes in my pocket. I'll mail it to the front desk tomorrow.

Outside, Manhattan is exactly herself. Loud, bright, relentless — a sensory overdose in every direction. A delivery bike cuts past us, close enough to feel the wind. A horn section from somewhere — a busker, a club, someone's open window — battles with the thump of a bass line from a passing car. The air smells like exhaust and roasted nuts and the particular charge of a city that never, ever stops performing. It's overwhelming and overstimulating and exactly as much as it always is, which is too much.
Julian takes my hand.

The grip is the same one he used on the boat in Cork Harbor, that day we went out past the harbor wall and the water got choppy and he held on without thinking about it. Unconscious and protective and warm. His fingers interlace with mine and his palm is rough and present and I feel the grip in my wrist, my forearm, my chest.
"You okay?" he asks.

I look at the city I used to call home. The skyline is all lit up — not the same buildings I used to see from Sterling's thirty-fourth floor, not exactly, because I'm not on the thirty-fourth floor anymore. I'm on the street. Ground level. Where the people are. Where the noise is. Where the life moves through you instead of below you.

I look at the world I could have chosen. The one where I stayed, kept the suits, worked the job, climbed the ladder. The one where I never met Julian in a kitchen in Clonakilty. The one where I never glitched, never forked, never fell sideways into a different version of everything.
And I feel — gratitude.

Not FOMO. Not the fear that I'm missing something. Not the ambient ache of a life half-lived because the other half is happening somewhere without me. Not the grasping, comparing, calculating panic that used to hum in my chest like bad wiring every time someone else's life looked shinier than mine.
Just gratitude. Clean and simple. Gratitude that I got to live here — really live here, in this ridiculous, exhausting, magnificent city — and gratitude that I get to leave.

Not running away. Not escaping. Just... done. The way you finish a really good book and close it and hold it for a moment before you set it on the shelf. You don't wish for more chapters. You don't resent the ending. You're just glad you read it.
"Yeah," I say. "I'm good."
Julian squeezes my hand and we walk toward the river, and Manhattan roars around us, and I don't look back.
